coredata.dc:title: Biomass-based carbon nanofibers enhanced by carbon quantum dots for high-performance supercapacitors and moist-electric generators
coredata.dc:creator: Jin, Zhenxing
Cao, Qiping
Gong, Hui
Chen, Bo
Jiang, Yuewei
Su, Yingying
Zhou, Jinghui
Li, Yao
coredata.dc:description: Developing a simple and effective method for optimizing lignin structure is a key challenge for the application of biomass-based carbon nanofibers in energy storage and moisture-enabled electricity generation fields. In this work, an effective strategy is designed to prepare novel biomass-based carbon nanofibers. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are introduced as functional components into biomass-based carbon nanofibers to optimize the inherent structural defects of lignin. The introduction of CQDs effectively reduces the interaction between lignin macromolecules, thereby increasing the flexibility of lignin macromolecule chain segments. The obtained biomass-based carbon nanofibers exhibit excellent microfiber morphologies and a high degree of graphitization. The specific capacitance and energy density for biomass-based carbon nanofibers as the energy storage device reach 294.4 F/g and 28.3 W h/kg, respectively. Furthermore, the biomass-based moist-electric generator shows high power generation efficiency, the output voltage and output current of a single device reach 0.75 V and 1.8 μA, respectively. Notably, as the number of biomass-based moist-electric generators in series or parallel increases, the overall output efficiency of the device system has a linear relationship, which means that a sufficient number of biomass-based moist-electric generator devices can meet the power supply requirements of larger power systems. This work puts forward a promising strategy to prepare low-consumption, high-performance, and environmentally friendly biomass-based carbon nanofibers for the supercapacitors and moist-electric generators.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Bio-renewable
Carbon nanofibers
Supercapacitor
Moist-electric generators

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of biomass-based carbon nanofiber embedded with carbon quantum dots
Physicochemical characterization of carbon quantum dots
Physicochemical properties of biomass-based nanofibers embedded with carbon quantum dots
Electrochemical analysis of biomass-based carbon nanofibers embedded with carbon quantum dots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22278048 , 31800498 , 22078035 , 21908014 ), Natural Science Foundation of Liaoning Province of China (No. LJKZ0543 ), Foundation (No. 2021KF07 ) of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control, College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Guangxi University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b) TEM image and high-resolution TEM image of the CQDs, (c) XRD pattern of the CQDs, (d) FT-IR spectra of the CQDs, (e) UV–Vis absorption of the CQDs, and (f) fluorescence emission spectra of the CQDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra, (b) TG curves, and (c) DSC curves of the BPFs prepared with CQDs, (d) the viscosity of the BPFs spinning solution with a different shear rate.
(a–e) SEM images of BCFs-1, BCFs-2, BCFs-3, BCFs-4 and BCFs-5 prepared with CQDs, and (f–g) TEM images of BCFs-4.
(a) BET and (b) BJH curves of BCFs prepared with CQDs; (c) XRD patterns and (d) Raman spectra of the BCFs prepared with CQDs.

(a–e) CV curves at scan rates of 5–200 mV/s of BCFs-1, BCFs-2, BCFs-3, BCFs-4, and BCFs-5; GCD curves of (f) BCFs-1, (g) BCFs-2, (h) BCFs-3, (i) BCFs-4, and (j) BCFs-5 at current density of 1 A/g, 2 A/g, 5 A/g, 10 A/g, and 20 A/g.

(a) CV curves at a scan rate of 200 mV/s, (b) GCD curves at current density of 1 A/g, and (c) EIS curves of BCFs; (d) CV curves at 100 mV/s, (e) GCD curves at 1 A/g of the electrode at different operation voltages, (f) CV curves at different scan rates from 10 to 200 mV/s, (g) GCD curves from 1 to 20 A/g, (h) gravimetric capacitances at different current densities, and (i) Ragone plots on the supercapacitor of BCFs measured in a double-electrode system.

(a) The assembly diagram of the moist-electric generator for BCFs, the output voltage (b) and current (c) of BCFs-4 moist-electric generator with a relative humidity variation of 75%, (d) the power generation efficiency of BCFs-4 moist-electric generator under different temperature environments, the output voltage (e) and current (f) of multiple BCFs-4 moist-electric generators, (g) a small diode lit by using a power supply system assembled from a BCFs-4 moist-electric generator.
Mechanism diagram of carbon nanofibers enhanced by carbon quantum dots.

head.ce:title: Biomass-based carbon nanofibers enhanced by carbon quantum dots for high-performance supercapacitors and moist-electric generators

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory of Pulp and Papermaking Engineering, Liaoning Key Lab of Lignocellulose Chemistry and BioMaterials, Liaoning Collaborative Innovation Center for Lignocellulosic Biorefinery, Dalian Polytechnic University, Dalian, Liaoning Province, 116034, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory of Pulp and Papermaking Engineering
Liaoning Key Lab of Lignocellulose Chemistry and BioMaterials
Liaoning Collaborative Innovation Center for Lignocellulosic Biorefinery

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory of Pulp and Papermaking Engineering, Liaoning Key Lab of Lignocellulose Chemistry and BioMaterials, Liaoning Collaborative Innovation Center for Lignocellulosic Biorefinery, Dalian Polytechnic University, Dalian, Liaoning Province, 116034, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this article.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing a simple and effective method for optimizing lignin structure is a key challenge for the application of biomass-based carbon nanofibers in energy storage and moisture-enabled electricity generation fields. In this work, an effective strategy is designed to prepare novel biomass-based carbon nanofibers. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are introduced as functional components into biomass-based carbon nanofibers to optimize the inherent structural defects of lignin. The introduction of CQDs effectively reduces the interaction between lignin macromolecules, thereby increasing the flexibility of lignin macromolecule chain segments. The obtained biomass-based carbon nanofibers exhibit excellent microfiber morphologies and a high degree of graphitization. The specific capacitance and energy density for biomass-based carbon nanofibers as the energy storage device reach 294.4 F/g and 28.3 W h/kg, respectively. Furthermore, the biomass-based moist-electric generator shows high power generation efficiency, the output voltage and output current of a single device reach 0.75 V and 1.8 μA, respectively. Notably, as the number of biomass-based moist-electric generators in series or parallel increases, the overall output efficiency of the device system has a linear relationship, which means that a sufficient number of biomass-based moist-electric generator devices can meet the power supply requirements of larger power systems. This work puts forward a promising strategy to prepare low-consumption, high-performance, and environmentally friendly biomass-based carbon nanofibers for the supercapacitors and moist-electric generators.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As the global environmental crisis and energy poverty worsen, the exploration and storage of clean energy sources have gradually attracted extensive attention [1–4]. Among various clean energy sources, moisture-enabled electricity generation, as a novel emerging energy-harvesting technology, is a promising candidate for supplying renewable and clean power [5–8]. The key component of a moist-electric generator is the porous structural material with various active functional groups on the surface [9,10]. Compared with the porous metal oxides and conductive polymers, carbon-based materials are regarded as the most ideal functional materials for the moist-electric generator because of their porous structure, modifiability, low cost and good cyclic stability [11–13]. Among the carbon-based materials, carbon nanofiber is a promising candidate for moisture-enabled electricity generation because of its various active functional groups, high conductivity, controllable fibrous morphology, excellent chemical stability, and high flexibility [14–17]. However, the traditional carbon nanofiber materials are made from polyacrylonitrile, an expensive and non-renewable polymer, limiting the further development and application of carbon nanofiber materials [18–20]. Therefore, improving the sustainability and economy of carbon nanofiber materials is a crucial challenge.

As a renewable resource with a wide range of sources, lignin has great application potential in the carbon-based materials field due to its sustainability, abundance of aromatic structures, and high carbon content [21–25]. Furthermore, the abundant phenolic hydroxyl, quinone, and other active groups provide the active sites for chemical modification and structural optimization of lignin [26,27]. These properties have aroused great interest in the preparation of lignin-based carbon nanofibers [28,29]. Although lignin-based carbon nanofibers have made great progress in the field of energy storage and electricity storage, their application is still limited by the low degree of graphitization and lack of active functional groups on the surface [30–32]. These performance defects are mainly attributed to the strong rigidity of the lignin macromolecule, which is an inherent chemical structural defect [33,34]. The strong rigidity is not conducive to the molecular conformational transformation of lignin macromolecules during high-temperature treatment, which results in a large spatial distance between lignin macromolecules and then hinders the accumulation and formation of graphitized structures within the lignin-based carbon nanofibers [35,36]. To solve this problem, a complex chemical modification process is usually necessary [37,38]. Reducing the intermolecular force of lignin macromolecules or increasing the flexible connection bond in the chemical structure through chemical modification can overcome the inherent structural defects of lignin [39,40]. However, this chemical modification strategy requires high experimental conditions and is not conducive to the large-scale production of lignin-based carbon nanofibers [41]. Therefore, developing a simple method for optimizing lignin structure has become a key challenge for the application of lignin-based carbon nanofibers in energy storage and moisture-enabled electricity generation fields.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are nanoscale zero-dimensional carbon materials with a large number of active groups on the surface [42–44]. The nanoscale volume allows CQDs to uniformly composite with various materials, thereby endowing the materials with diverse functionalities [45,46]. Furthermore, a large number of active groups on the surface provide the possibility of chemical modification and covalent bond insertion for CQDs [47–50]. In this work, an effective strategy is designed to prepare novel biomass-based carbon nanofibers. The reaction mechanism is shown in Scheme 1 . CQDs are introduced as functional components into biomass-based carbon nanofibers to optimize the inherent structural defects of lignin. The introduction of CQDs effectively reduces the interaction between lignin macromolecules, thereby increasing the flexibility of lignin macromolecule chain segments. The obtained biomass-based carbon nanofibers exhibit excellent microfiber morphologies and a high degree of graphitization. Furthermore, a large number of active functional groups exist on the biomass-based carbon nanofibers surface due to the uniform distribution of CQDs, which is beneficial for improving energy storage and humidity power generation performance. The biomass-based carbon nanofibers achieve a specific capacitance of 294.4 F/g. With the 640 W/kg of power density, the energy density is 28.3 W h/kg. The biomass-based moist-electric generator shows high power generation efficiency, the output voltage and output current of a single device reach 0.75 V and 1.8 μA, respectively. Notably, as the number of biomass-based moist-electric generators in series or parallel increases, the overall output voltage and current of the device system have a linear relationship, which means that a sufficient number of biomass-based moist-electric generator devices can meet the power supply requirements of larger power systems. This work puts forward a promising strategy to prepare low-consumption, high-performance, and environmentally friendly biomass-based carbon nanofibers for the supercapacitors and moist-electric generators.

The poplar powder was provided by Yue Yang Forest & Paper Co. Cellulose acetate (CA) and ethylenediamine were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Phosphoric, hydrochloric, and citric acid were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Corporation. Acetone and N, N-dimethylacetamide (DMAc) were supplied by Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. All chemicals were used as received.

100 g of poplar powder and 60 wt% ethanol solution were added to an electrothermal cooking pot (ZQS-15 type, Shanxi University of Science and Technology Machinery Factory, China) and heated at 200 °C for 120 min. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was filtered and washed with 50% ethanol (200 mL). The filtrates were poured into 600 mL of water, and then HCl was dropped into the mixture until pH = 2. The black precipitate obtained after standing was repeatedly washed with hydrochloric acid to remove excess impurities. Finally, the poplar lignin powder was obtained after freeze-drying.

The carbon quantum dots were prepared by a simple, bottom-up, and hydrothermal method. Firstly, 1.05 g of citric acid powder and 10 mL of deionized water were stirred and dissolved in a PTFE tank. Subsequently, 335 μL of ethylenediamine was transferred into the above solution and reacted at 220 °C for 32 h. Finally, the golden yellow solid was obtained and named carbon quantum dots (CQDs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of biomass-based carbon nanofiber embedded with carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 0.1 g of CQDs was dissolved in 0.4 g of phosphoric acid solution and stirred for 1 h at 60 °C. After dissolving, the above solution was added with 5 g of DMAC and 5 g of acetone. Subsequently, 1 g of poplar lignin and 1 g of CA were sequentially added to the aforementioned mixture and agitated at 30 °C for 8 h to form the spinning solution. The electrospinning process was performed with a working distance of 20 cm, a propulsion rate of 0.8 mL/h, and a voltage of 24 kV. The biomass-based precursor fibers (BPFs) were collected and dried in a vacuum for 4 h at 60 °C. The obtained BPFs were heated in a muffle furnace with a heating rate of 0.5 °C/min and sustained for 4 h at 220 °C to produce biomass-based stabilized fibers. Subsequently, the biomass-based stabilized fibers were heated in a tube furnace from 50 to 800 °C at a rate of 2.0 °C/min while under a N2 environment to obtain the biomass-based carbon fibers (BCFs).

Five kinds of BPFs samples were prepared according to the content of CQDs in the spinning solution: BPFs-1 (the content of CQDs = 0 %), BPFs-2 (the content of CQDs = 0.8 %), BPFs-3 (the content of CQDs = 1.6 %), BPFs-4 (the content of CQDs = 2.4 %), and BPFs-5 (the content of CQDs = 3.2 %). BCFs were produced by heating at 800 °C for 3 h and named BCFs-1, BCFs-2, BCFs-3, BCFs-4, and BCFs-5, respectively.

The functional groups of each samples were characterized by FTIR spectrometer (PEINC., USA) with a spectral range of 4000 to 400 cm−1. Each BCFs was subjected to a thermal stability test using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA; TA Instruments Q500) in a N2 environment with a 10 °C/min heating rate from room temperature to 700 °C. The TA Discovery DSC 250 measuring instrument was used to perform a differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) analysis with a heating rate of 10 °C/min. The surface area and porosity were tested using the multi-point Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area technique. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was used to analyze and characterize the microstructure of CQDs and BCFs. Shimadzu XRD-7000 S diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation was used to conduct X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis on the phase structure, and a Microscopes Raman Spectrometer (Ranishaw Co., UK) was used to assess the carbon structure.

The working electrodes were constructed of polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE), carbon black, and BCFs in the following proportions: 1:2:7. The slurry (1 mg) was applied to the precleaned foam nickel (1 × 1 cm) and dried for 24 h in a vacuum atmosphere at 80 °C and 10 MPa of pressure. With 6 M KOH solution as the electrolyte, platinum coil as the counter electrode, Hg/HgO electrode as the reference electrode employed directly, the electrochemical performance of a three-electrode system was studied. Electrochemical properties measurements including cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) experiments of BCFs were conducted with a potentiostat on an electrochemical work station (CHI, 660D). The capacitance of electrode (Ce, F/g) from GCD was calculated by the following equation:
(1)
C e
=
I Δ t
m Δ V
Where I is the constant current (A), Δt is the discharge time (s), m is the mass of active materials (g), and ΔV represents the voltage window (V).

The energy and power densities of the device were calculated from the discharge curves at different current densities calculated by the following equations:
(2)
E =
C d
V 2
7.2
(3)
P =
3600
E
Δ t
Where E (W h/kg) is the energy density, Cd (F/g) is the specific capacitance of the device, V (V) is the potential drop during the discharge process, P (W/kg) is the power density, and Δt (s) is the discharge time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical characterization of carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: In order to preliminarily investigate the particle size and dispersion of the prepared CQDs, the micromorphology of the CQDs is observed by TEM. The TEM image and high-resolution TEM image of the obtained CQDs are shown in Fig. 1 a and b, respectively. The CQDs exhibit a small diameter of 2–4 nm and good monodispersion without agglomeration, implying that the CQDs have a good promotion effect on the preparation of spinning solutions with homogeneity and high solubility. Furthermore, distinct lattice fringes are observed in the high-resolution TEM image of CQDs. The interplanar spacing reaches about 0.2 nm. The crystallization behavior of CQDs is further characterized by the XRD pattern (Fig. 1c). The XRD result of CQDs demonstrates that a broad peak is observed centered at approximately 22.6° with an interlayer spacing of 0.365 nm, which is close to that of graphite (0.34 nm). The high-resolution TEM image and XRD result of CQDs suggest that the prepared CQDs have a good carbon graphitization structure, which can effectively induce the graphitization behavior of lignin macromolecules during carbonization treatment.

The chemical structures of CQDs are analyzed by FT-IR spectra, UV–vis absorption spectrum, and photoluminescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 1d, the stretching vibration peaks of the –CH3 and –CH2 are around 2900 cm−1. The stretching vibration peaks of –OH and –NH are observed at 3432 cm−1. The typical stretching vibration peak at 1714 cm−1 is attributed to the –CO. The typical absorption peaks of amide structures are at 1655 and 1545 cm−1, which are attributed to the amide I bands and the amide II bands produced by the amide N–H bending and C–N stretching vibrations, respectively. The UV–vis absorption spectrum is performed to further investigate the conjugated structural group of CQD (Fig. 1e). Obviously, two strong absorption bands are observed in the UV–vis absorption spectrum. The UV–vis absorption peak at 339 nm is due to the n-π* electronic transitions of the p–π conjugated structures on CQDs surfaces. The UV–vis absorption peak for the π-π* electronic transitions of conjugated organic molecules is observed at 225 nm. Fig. 1f shows the photoluminescence spectra of CQDs obtained by excitation with different wavelengths. The photoluminescence behavior of CQDs is related to the excitation wavelength, which is observed at 320–480 nm. With the increase in excitation wavelength, the emission peak of CQDs increases from 510 to 550 nm (a red shift). The typical excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior of CQDs is attributed to the multiple discrete electronic states, implying that various active functional groups are present on the surface of CQDs, especially chemical groups containing p-electron cloud structures. These characterizations indicate that the prepared CQDs are nanoscale carbon materials with graphitization structures, various surface functional groups, and good monodispersion without agglomeration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical properties of biomass-based nanofibers embedded with carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2 a, the functional groups of BCFs are characterized by FT-IR spectra. The strong characteristic absorption peaks around 3435 cm−1 are attributed to the stretch vibrations of –OH and –NH. With the increase of CQDs content, the peak intensity becomes stronger, indicating that CQDs are successfully embedded. Interestingly, the absorption peak intensity of CO at 1745 cm−1 also shows a similar increase. The stretching vibration peaks at 2924 cm−1 and 2850 cm−1 are credited to –CH3 and –CH2, respectively. The peaks at 1619, 1516, and 1463 cm−1 have not changed significantly, showing typical aromatic skeleton vibration peaks. The thermal properties of biomass-based precursor fibers are crucial in the heat treatment process of carbon materials. Fig. 2b shows the TGA curves for analyzing the thermal properties of BPFs in an N2 atmosphere. The thermal stability (starting temperature of thermal weight loss) of BPFs gradually increases with the increase of CQDs content from 0 to 2.4 wt%. This result is mainly attributed to the successful embedding of CQDs, which improves the thermal stability of BCFs. However, the thermal stability of BPFs decreases significantly while the content of CQDs reaches 3.2 wt%, which is caused by the aggregation of CQDs. This aggregation leads to an uneven distribution and phase separation of substances inside the spinning solution. The carbon yields of BPFs-1, BPFs-2, BPFs-3, BPFs-4, and BPFs-5 are 41.4, 41.9, 43.4, 49.6, and 44.4%, respectively. The carbon yield results show a similar trend to the thermal stability of BCFs. Outstanding thermal stability and high carbon yield are of great significance for the morphologies, graphitization degree, and specific surface area of BCFs.

The flexibility of the macromolecule chain segment is reflected by measuring the glass transition temperature (Tg). The thermal behavior of BPFs is further analyzed by DSC curves (Fig. 2c). The Tg shows a gradual decreasing trend with the increase of CQDs content. This phenomenon is primarily attributed to the abundant active groups on CQDs surfaces, which promote interaction between biomass and CQDs, reduce phase separation and endow the macromolecule chain segment with good flexibility. However, the Tg increases slightly while the content of CQDs reaches 3.2 wt%. This may be due to the aggregation of CQDs, which weakens the movement of the macromolecule chain segments.

The viscosity and rheological characteristics of the BPFs spinning solution have a direct impact on the morphology and quality of electrospun nanofibers. In order to discuss the rheology of the BPFs spinning solution, the relationship between viscosity and shear rate is characterized and analyzed. As shown in Fig. 2d, the viscosity of the BPFs spinning solutions reduces obviously with the increase in shear rate, which indicates that all BPFs spinning solutions exhibit the typical shear behavior of non-Newtonian fluids. Moreover, the viscosity of BPFs-5 spinning solutions (the content of CQDs: 3.2 wt%) is significantly higher than that of other spinning solutions. This result indicates that the introduction of CQDs effectively increases the interaction between biomass and CQDs in the spinning solution. Generally, the increase in fluid viscosity is mainly due to the internal dynamic enhancement and the high entanglement of macromolecule chain segments. However, the high viscosity prevents electrostatic forces from stretching the spinning solution into nanofibers, and the spinning solution will not be able to form fibers in time to fall as small droplets, forming “bead-like structural defects".

As shown in Fig. 3 a, the morphologies of BCFs are observed by SEM and TEM. The BCFs-1 surface is smooth, the diameter is uniform, and there are no obvious “bead-like structural defects”. With the introduction of CQDs, the diameter of BCFs decreases gradually, which may be due to the effective reduction of strong interactions between lignin macromolecules, improving the spinnability of spinning solutions. However, the bead-like structural defects gradually appear on the BCFs surface with the increase of CQDs content (Fig. 3b–d). In particular, a large number of bead-like structural defects appear in BCFs-5 (the content of CQDs: 3.2 wt%) (Fig. 3e). This may be due to the aggregation of CQDs, which reduces the viscosity and spinnability of the spinning solution. BCFs-4 is further characterized by TEM to analyze the internal morphology. As shown in Fig. 3f and g, the surface of BCFs-4 is embedded with many CQDs. As the external protection of the nanofiber, this structure can reduce heat loss during heat treatment and promote the attachment of the electrolyte ions. Moreover, it is beneficial to obtain biomass-based carbon materials with a high specific surface area.

The specific surface area and pore size distribution of BCFs embedded with CQDs are analyzed by N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms (Fig. 4 a and b). All BCFs samples show H3 hysteresis loops and type IV isotherms, indicating the characteristics of microporous materials and the slit pore structure of sheet structure stacking. In addition, the isotherm shows a sharp increase in N2 absorption at higher relative pressures (P/P0 > 0.9), indicating the presence of the macroporous structure. The microporous structure is produced in the processes of preoxidation and carbonization, providing satisfactory charge regulation ability. The high specific surface area provided by mesopores and macropores is beneficial to improving the ion transport of BCFs as supercapacitor electrodes in the process of charge storage. The specific surface areas of BCFs-1, BCFs-2, BCFs-3, BCFs-4, and BCFs-5 reach 452.5, 515.8, 600.4, 835.5, and 702.4 m2/g, respectively. Obviously, BCFs-4 exhibits the highest specific surface area compared to other BCFs, which is beneficial to obtain supercapacitors with high storage capacity. In addition, the specific surface areas of the BCFs initially increase and then decrease with the increase of CQDs content. This phenomenon is consistent with the microscopic morphology analysis of BCFs.

In order to further analyze the internal structure and graphitization degree of BCFs, X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) and Raman spectroscopy are performed. As shown in Fig. 4c, two weak broad peaks around 2θ of 25° and 43° are indexed to the (002) and (101) crystallographic planes. According to the Bragg equation, the average interlayer spacing d002 of BCFs-1, BCFs-2, BCFs-3, BCFs-4, and BCFs-5 is 0.374, 0.369, 0.358, 0.345, and 0.378 nm, respectively. The narrower lattice spacing implies a tighter graphitized structure. BCFs-4 exhibits the narrowest lattice spacing closest to the graphene lattice (0.335 nm) compared to other BCFs. As shown in Fig. 4d, all BCFs samples show two peaks at 1350 (D-band) cm−1 and 1594 cm−1 (G-band). These two bands represent the results of disordered carbon structure and the existence of an ordered graphite lattice, respectively. Obviously, the strength of the G-band of the BCFs-1, BCFs-2, BCFs-3, and BCFs-4 is stronger than that of the D-band, indicating a high graphitization degree. The degree of structural defects in carbon-based materials is usually evaluated by the strength ratio between the D-band and the G-band (ID/IG). The calculated ID/IG values of the BCFs-1, BCFs-2, BCFs-3, BCFs-4, and BCFs-5 are 0.85, 0.84, 0.71, 0.66, and 1.12, respectively. The BCFs-4 shows the lowest R value compared to other BCFs, indicating a better graphitized structure. This is mainly attributed to the introduction of CQDs with graphitization structures, which can induce the graphitization process of biomass during heat treatment. However, excessive CQDs embedded in the carbon skeleton will cause phase separation, resulting in structural defects and a low degree of graphitization in BCFs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical analysis of biomass-based carbon nanofibers embedded with carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) are performed to evaluate the electrochemical capacitance of BCFs embedded with CQDs in a 6 M KOH electrolyte. The CV curves of BCFs are obtained in a three-electrode system at scanning rates of 5–200 mV/s. As shown in Fig. 5 a–e, near-rectangular shapes with almost no distortion are observed in the CV curves of all BCFs samples, which is attributed to the superior rate capability and good conductivity. In particular, BCFs-4 shows a more rectangle-like shape and the largest closed scanning area compared to other BCFs, which highlights its excellent capacitive behavior and good reversibility.

The GCD curves are performed to compare the more detailed electrochemical capacitance of BCFs in a three-electrode system. As shown in Fig. 5f–j, all BCFs samples embedded with CQDs show excellent capacitive properties, which is similar to the CV measurements. At low current densities, all GCD curves exhibit symmetrical triangles, indicating good capacitive characteristics. The symmetric triangular shape of the GCD curves remains unchanged at a high current density, which is attributed to the excellent reversible energy storage capabilities of BCFs. As three-electrode capacitances, the specific capacitances of BCFs-1, BCFs-2, BCFs-3, BCFs-4, and BCFs-5 are 159.4, 181.7, 211.5, 294.4, and 247.4 F/g, respectively. BCFs-4 exhibits the best electrochemical energy storage behavior and charge discharge results compared to other BCFs. The high specific capacitance of BCFs is attributed to the fact that the GQDs-modified carbon skeleton provides more attachment sites and channels for electrons.

The CV and GCD curves are measured at a high scanning speed and low current density to intuitively compare the electrochemical performance of BCFs. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the closing area of all BCFs samples initially increases and then decreases with the increase of CQDs content. Among them, BCFs-4 shows the largest closing area. From Fig. 6b, all GCD curves show approximately isosceles triangles at a low current density. BCFs-4 has a longer discharge time compared to other BCFs, which indicates a greater capacity to store charges. The key to creating high-performance supercapacitors is having low series equivalent resistance. Fig. 6c depicts the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements of BCFs to further discuss the process of electron transfer. The excellent capacitance behavior and low charge transfer resistance (Rct) of BCFs are indicated by the narrow semicircle in the high-frequency zone and the high slope in the low-frequency area. Comparing with other BCFs, BCFs-4 exhibits the lowest equivalent series resistance (Rs) and Rct, which are credited to the high crystallinity of CQDs and their uniform distribution on the fiber surface.

Using 1.0 mol/L Na2SO4, the energy storage performance of BCFs-4 as positive and negative electrodes is further evaluated as the electrolyte in a double-electrode system. Fig. 6d and e displays the CV curves (100 mV/s) and the GCD curves (1 A/g) of BCFs-4, respectively. The approximate rectangle shape in the CV curve indicates the excellent energy storage performance of EDLC, and the approximate linear curve in the symmetrical triangle shape in the GCD curves is an attribute of the high reversibility of BCFs-4. With different scan rates and current densities, the CV curves (5–200 mV/s) and the GCD curves (1–10 A/g) are shown in Fig. 6f and g, respectively. The CV curves of BCFs-4 at different scan rates show a quasi-rectangular shape. Even at a scan rate of 200 mV/s, the CV curves of BCFs-4 still maintain a quasi-rectangular shape at a low potential with a slight offset, indicating a faster charge propagation speed. The voltage-time curves of BCFs-4 show an ideal linear shape. According to the discharge curves, when the current density is 1 A/g, the specific capacitance of the BCFs-4 electrode reaches 78.6 F/g. This result is superior to most other porous carbon materials under the same current. As shown in Fig. 6h, BCFs-4 maintains an excellent specific capacitance of 75 F/g at high current densities. Even with a current density of 20 A/g, the capacitance of BCFs-4 still remains at 81.4%. The two key parameters for evaluating the entire EDLC device are energy density and power density. Fig. 6i shows the Ragone plot of BCFs-4. When the power density is 640 W/kg, BCFs-4 reaches the maximum energy density of 28.3 W h/kg. BCFs-4 shows outstanding energy and power density due to the various porous structures, large specific surface area, abundant active sites, and fast charge exchange. Moreover, the BCFs-4 electrode exhibits excellent stability, with 99.8% capacitance retention (Fig. S3a) and a Coulombic efficiency (Fig. S3b) of 100% after 10,000 cycles at 1 Ag−1.

The fibrous morphology and diverse surface active groups of BCFs have benefits for improving the properties of the moist-electric generator. To further investigate the power generation properties of BCFs in humid environments, BCFs-4 is sandwiched by two pieces of aluminum electrodes (Fig. 7 a), and an insulated sponge is inserted between the aluminum electrode and BCFs-4. Subsequently, this moist-electric generator is placed in a closed constant humidity environment. With the gradual approach of water molecules and BCFs-4, the charges ionized by the water molecules are triggered and induced by the active functional groups distributed on the surface of BCFs-4. These free charges will spread with the diffusion of water molecules towards the bottom electrode. During this process, the free charges will gradually transfer to BCFs-4 through the carrier mechanism under the concentration difference. Due to the fibrous morphology of the ideal nanoscale channels, this BCFs-4 moist-electric generator presents a typical electrokinetic phenomenon caused by ionizable solutions driving through narrow channels under a pressure gradient. These free charges gradually accumulate on the surface of BCFs-4 and form a charge distribution on the surface of the vertical channel, which is called the double layer. Finally, as water molecules evaporate, the water inside BCFs-4 continuously flows, resulting in a relatively stable potential. As shown in Fig. 7b and c, with a relative humidity variation of 75%, this BCFs-4 moist-electric generator could provide a voltage and current output of ca. 0.65 V and ca. 1.8 μA. It is obvious that the output voltage gradually increases with the increase in experimental testing time, which is mainly attributed to the gradual increase in water molecule content accumulated within the BCFs-4 moist-electric generator. On the contrary, the output current shows a significant decrease and then tends to stabilize (Fig. 7c). This decrease in output current is mainly attributed to the increased distance between electrodes caused by the introduction of water molecules, thereby increasing the internal resistance of the moist-electric generator. Subsequently, the power generation properties of the BCFs-4 moist-electric generator in different external environments are further characterized. As shown in Fig. 7d, the BCFs-4 moist-electric generator shows different output voltage under different temperature environments. At 25 °C, the BCFs-4 moist-electric generator shows the highest output voltage, and the output voltage reaches 0.75 V. With the increase or decrease of temperature, the output voltage decreases obviously, which is attributed to the restriction of water molecular motion and the damage of molecular Brownian motion to the formation of an electric double layer. Finally, multiple BCFs-4 moist-electric generators are connected in series or parallel to test the stability of the output voltage and current (Fig. 7e and f). Obviously, the series or parallel process does not affect the stability of the output voltage and current for the BCFs-4 moist-electric generator. The output voltage of five series connected BCFs-4 moist-electric generators reaches 3.75 V, and the output current of five parallel-connected BCFs-4 moist-electric generators reaches 7 μA. It is worth noting that as the number of BCFs-4 moist-electric generators in series or parallel increases, the overall output voltage and current of the device system have a linear relationship, which means that a sufficient number of BCFs-4 moist-electric generator devices can meet the power supply requirements of larger power systems. Furthermore, a small diode is lit using a power supply system assembled from a BCFs-4 moist-electric generator (Fig. 7g).

Summary, the introduction of CQDs effectively optimizes the inherent structural defects of lignin and reduces the interaction between lignin macromolecules, thereby increasing the flexibility of lignin macromolecule chain segments. The obtained biomass-based carbon nanofibers exhibit excellent microfiber morphologies and a high degree of graphitization. The specific capacitance and energy density for biomass-based carbon nanofibers as the energy storage device reach 294.4 F/g and 28.3 W h/kg, respectively. Furthermore, the biomass-based moist-electric generator shows high power generation efficiency, the output voltage and output current of a single device reach 0.75 V and 1.8 μA, respectively. With the increase in the number of biomass-based moist-electric generator in series or parallel, the overall output efficiency of the device system has a linear relationship. This result suggests that a sufficient number of biomass-based moist-electric generator devices can meet the power supply requirements of larger power systems. This work puts forward a promising strategy to prepare low-consumption, high-performance, and environmentally friendly biomass-based carbon nanofibers for the supercapacitors and moist-electric generators.

Zhenxing Jin: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Qiping Cao: Methodology, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Hui Gong: Writing – review & editing. Bo Chen: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Yuewei Jiang: Writing – review & editing. Yingying Su: Writing – review & editing. Jinghui Zhou: Investigation, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Yao Li: Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22278048, 31800498, 22078035, 21908014), Natural Science Foundation of Liaoning Province of China (No. LJKZ0543), Foundation (No. 2021KF07) of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control, College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Guangxi University.
